title: Two-Person Picnic Table
summary: A face-to-face picnic table for two — one shared seat frame and a cantilevered top on angled legs, sized for a balcony, small patio, or morning coffee for a couple.
description:
A full picnic table seats eight and owns the whole yard. This one seats two people across from each other and fits where a bistro set would — but it's built like a picnic table: 2x6 top and seats, 2x4 frames, angled legs, all screwed together with exterior fasteners. It's the picnic table idea at espresso scale.

The structure is smarter than it looks. The seat frame and tabletop frame are separate assemblies on their own 30°-angled legs, joined to each other at the back — the table half leans on the seat half, and a single 45°-beveled 2x6 cross support under the tabletop ties the cantilever back into the base. That cross support is not optional trim: without it, the table half hinges on its connection every time someone leans on the far edge.

The fussiest moment is step 7, and it's an alignment problem, not a cutting problem: the tabletop assembly and seat assembly have to be held in position — seat level, top level, back stringer tight — while you drive the connecting screws. A second person or a wall to lean against turns a frustrating step into a two-minute one. Check level on both surfaces BEFORE the four-screws-per-joint go in, because after them, it's pry-bar territory.

The optional dog-ear corner cuts on the frame ends are worth the extra ten minutes — on a table this small, the frame corners sit exactly at knee height.

tools.note: The 30° leg cuts and the 45° bevels on the cross support.
Straight cuts and the optional dog-ear corners.
Marks the dog-ears and keeps the frames square.

materials.name: 2x6, 10 ft
materials.note: Top, seats, and legs. Pick straight ones — the legs are cut from these.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends parallel.
Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends parallel.
Both ends beveled 45°, ends NOT parallel. This brace is what makes the cantilevered top rigid.

steps.title: Build the tabletop frame
steps.body: Screw the two inner supports between the two outer supports — 2-1/2" screws, two per joint. If you want the softened look, mark dog-ear cuts on the outer corners with a speed square and take them off with the circular saw now, while the frame is easy to handle.

steps.title: Deck the tabletop
steps.body:
Lay the four 2x6 top boards across the frame — ends flush with the outer supports, 1-1/2" overhang each side, gaps evened out. Screw down.

Set the gaps with spacers (a stack of washers works) rather than by eye; four boards make any drift obvious.

steps.title: Attach the table legs
steps.body: Screw the two long 30° legs to the tabletop supports, three screws per joint. The parallel 30° ends mean the leg leans while both the floor end and the frame end sit flush — orient both legs leaning the same direction.

steps.title: Build the seat frame
steps.body: Assemble the seat frame — the 60" back stringer, the two seat supports, and the two short front stringers. Same screws, same optional dog-ears on the exposed corners, which here sit right at calf height.

steps.title: Deck the seat and add its legs
steps.body: Screw the four seat boards to the seat frame with even gaps, then attach the two short 30° front legs.

steps.title: Join the two assemblies
steps.body:
Stand the tabletop assembly and seat assembly together — this is the have-a-helper step, or back the pieces against a wall. Check that the seat is level, the top is level, and the long stringer is seated tight against the table structure. Adjust until all three are true.

Then commit: 2-1/2" screws, four per joint. Everything about how this table sits is decided right here.

steps.title: Install the cross support and finish
steps.body:
Fit the 45°-beveled 2x6 cross support under the tabletop. Screw through the sides of the tabletop supports into it, and from the back side of the stringer into the end of the tabletop support.

This brace closes the structural triangle — push down hard on the table's front edge before and after installing it and you'll feel exactly what it does. Sand everything, ease the edges, and stain.

images.alt: Small two-person picnic table with facing seats built from 2x6 lumber
